section.title: The Bride's Longing
section.sectionRationale: The Song opens by naming itself the Song of Songs, the best of songs, and plunges straight into the voice of a woman longing for her beloved. The movement runs from her opening cry for his kisses, through a candid confession that she feels weathered and unworthy, to a mounting exchange of praise between the lovers, and it closes with the first sounding of the refrain that will guard the whole book: do not awaken love until the time is right.
section.thesis: Love that is delighted in, spoken openly, and rightly ordered is a good gift of God; the bride longs for her beloved, finds herself desired in return, and learns that such love is to be awakened in its proper time.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the bride's opening longing and her confession that she is dark yet lovely; the growing exchange of praise between the bride, the bridegroom, and the friends; and the bride at rest in his embrace, sounding the refrain, do not arouse or awaken love until the time is right.

units.label: Longing and the dark yet lovely bride

verses.text: This is Solomon’s Song of Songs.
Let him kiss me with the kisses of his mouth! For your {{ahavah|love}} is more delightful than wine.
The fragrance of your perfume is pleasing; your name is like perfume poured out. No wonder the maidens adore you.
Take me away with you, let us hurry! May the king bring me to his chambers. We will rejoice and delight in you; we will praise your love more than wine. It is only right that they adore you.
I am dark, yet lovely, O daughters of Jerusalem, like the tents of Kedar, like the curtains of Solomon.
Do not stare because I am dark, for the sun has gazed upon me. My mother’s sons were angry with me; they made me a keeper of the vineyards, but my own vineyard I have neglected.
Tell me, O one I love, where do you pasture your sheep? Where do you rest them at midday? Why should I be like a veiled woman beside the flocks of your companions?
If you do not know, O fairest of women, follow the tracks of the flock, and graze your young goats near the tents of the shepherds.
I compare you, my {{raya|darling}}, to a mare among Pharaoh’s chariots.
Your cheeks are beautiful with ornaments, your neck with strings of jewels.
We will make you ornaments of gold, studded with beads of silver.
While the king was at his table, my perfume spread its fragrance.
My {{dodi|beloved}} is to me a sachet of myrrh resting between my breasts.
My beloved is to me a cluster of henna blossoms in the vineyards of En-gedi.
How beautiful you are, my darling! Oh, how very beautiful! Your eyes are like doves.
How handsome you are, my beloved! Oh, how delightful! The soft grass is our bed.
The beams of our house are cedars; our rafters are fragrant firs.

units.label: At rest, and the refrain
units.note: do not awaken love too soon

verses.text: I am a rose of Sharon, a lily of the valley.
Like a lily among the thorns is my darling among the maidens.
Like an apple tree among the trees of the forest is my beloved among the young men. I delight to sit in his shade, and his fruit is sweet to my taste.
He has brought me to the house of wine, and his banner over me is love.
Sustain me with raisins; refresh me with apples, for I am faint with love.
His left hand is under my head, and his right arm embraces me.
O daughters of Jerusalem, I adjure you by the gazelles and does of the field: Do not arouse or awaken {{ahavah|love}} until the time is right.

ahavah.gloss: Love. The same word Scripture uses for love of God and neighbor. In the Song it is delighted, exclusive love between the man and the woman, more delightful than wine, and by 8:6 it is declared as strong as death.

raya.gloss: My darling, literally my companion or dear friend, the bridegroom’s recurring name for the bride. It joins tender affection to genuine friendship: love that is also companionship.

dodi.gloss: Beloved, the bride’s characteristic name for her man, my beloved, dodi. The root dod carries the sense of love and the loved one; it recurs like a refrain and expresses glad, exclusive belonging.

basic.context: The book calls itself the Song of Songs, the finest of all songs, and opens with a woman longing aloud for the man she loves. She admits she feels weathered and overlooked, dark from working in the sun, yet she calls herself lovely. The lovers begin to praise each other openly, and she rests in his embrace. Three times in the book, and first here, she warns the young women not to stir up love before its proper time. The plain sense is the beauty and dignity of love between a man and a woman.
basic.hermeneutics: The big question every reader asks is what kind of book this is. Is it a celebration of married love, an allegory of God’s love for His people, or both? The wisest answer holds them together: it truly sings the goodness of covenant love between husband and wife, and because God designed marriage as a living picture of Christ and the church, that love points beyond itself. Read this way, the Song is neither crude nor merely spiritualized away.
basic.originalLanguages: Two pet names run through the Song like refrains. The woman calls the man dodi, my beloved; the man calls her raya, my darling, a word that also means my companion or friend. The love itself is ahavah, the ordinary Hebrew word for love, the same used for loving God and neighbor. Here it is more delightful than wine.
basic.typology: Marriage was made from the beginning as a sign, the two becoming one flesh, Genesis 2. Paul says that mystery refers to Christ and the church, Ephesians 5. So the longing, the delight, and the belonging of these lovers picture, without being reduced to, the greater love of Christ for His people.
basic.christ: The bride’s cry, let him kiss me, her sense of being unworthy yet loved, and her rest under his banner of love, all echo the gospel: Christ loves and welcomes a people who feel unlovely, and His banner over them is love. The best of songs finally sings of the best of loves, the Bridegroom who gave Himself for His bride.
basic.god: God is the giver of love and delight. He is not embarrassed by the joy of husband and wife; He wrote it into creation and preserved this song in Scripture. He also teaches love’s right ordering, that it be awakened in its proper time.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 2:24 gives the one flesh union the Song celebrates. Ephesians 5:31 to 32 reveals marriage as a sign of Christ and the church. Proverbs 5:18 to 19 commends delight in one’s spouse.
basic.oneStory: The Song opens the door on a good gift often treated with suspicion: the love of a man and a woman. Held rightly, it honors that love and lets it point higher, to the God who is love and to Christ who loves His bride.

context.literary: The Song is Hebrew love poetry, rich in imagery drawn from gardens, spices, flocks, and vineyards, carried mostly in the alternating voices of the bride, the bridegroom, and a chorus called the friends or daughters of Jerusalem. This opening movement sets the pattern: longing, confession, mutual praise, and the recurring refrain of 2:7.
context.historical: Attributed to Solomon, the Song sits among Israel’s wisdom writings. Its images belong to the world of the ancient Near East, royal chambers, the tents of Kedar, the vineyards of En-gedi, yet its theme of faithful love is timeless.
context.canonical: As Scripture, the Song affirms the goodness of the body and of married love against every attempt to treat them as shameful. Read within the whole canon, its picture of covenant love joins the Bible’s larger marriage story, from Eden to the marriage supper of the Lamb.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To sing the goodness, beauty, and right ordering of covenant love between a man and a woman, giving voice to longing, delight, and belonging, and to guard that love with the refrain not to awaken it before its time.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The poem largely describes the experience of love rather than issuing commands, yet the thrice repeated refrain, do not arouse or awaken love until the time is right, is a genuine charge to honor love’s proper season.

debates.question: What kind of book is the Song: a celebration of married love, an allegory of Christ and the church, or both?

views.label: A celebration of covenant married love
views.case: The plain sense throughout is the mutual love of a man and a woman. The imagery, the named lovers, and the refrain about love’s right time all fit human marriage, and Scripture elsewhere openly commends such delight, Proverbs 5 and Genesis 2.

views.label: An allegory of Christ and the church, or God and Israel
views.case: For much of church history the Song was read as picturing God’s love for His people and Christ’s love for the church. This honors the many places where God calls Himself Israel’s husband, Hosea 2, and where Christ is the Bridegroom, Ephesians 5 and Revelation 19.

views.label: Both, the literal love as a God-given sign
views.case: The strongest reading holds the two together: the Song really celebrates married love, and because God made marriage as a picture of Christ and the church, Ephesians 5:31 to 32, that love rightly points beyond itself without being emptied of its plain meaning.

debates.bearing: Reading it as both keeps the Song honest and reverent: it neither reduces it to mere romance nor spiritualizes away the goodness of the body and of married love. It celebrates the gift and follows the gift to the Giver.

study.originalLanguages: From the first lines the Song hands us its two great pet names: dodi, my beloved, the woman’s word for the man, and raya, my darling or companion, the man’s word for the woman. The love between them is ahavah, the same word used for love of God and neighbor, here more delightful than wine. The woman’s striking confession, I am dark, yet lovely, holds together honest weariness and true beauty, the very tension the gospel later resolves for the church, unlovely in herself yet lovely in her Beloved.

typology.type: The bride who is dark yet lovely, unworthy yet desired
typology.fulfillment: The church, sinful in herself yet beautiful in Christ, loved and welcomed by the Bridegroom, Ephesians 5:25 to 27.

typology.type: His banner over me is love
typology.fulfillment: The open, protecting love of Christ over His people, under which they rest, Romans 8:38 to 39.

typology.type: The one flesh delight of the lovers
typology.fulfillment: The great mystery of Christ and the church that marriage was made to signify, Ephesians 5:31 to 32.

study.christ: The Song’s plain celebration of married love becomes, in the light of the whole Bible, a window onto the love of Christ. The bride who feels dark and overlooked yet is called lovely pictures the church, unworthy in herself yet beautiful in her Beloved. Her cry to be brought near, her rest under his banner of love, and her being desired before she is lovely all echo the gospel: Christ loved His bride and gave Himself for her, that He might present her radiant. The best of songs finally sings of the best of loves.
study.god: God is revealed here as the generous author of love and delight, who is not ashamed of the joy of husband and wife but wrote it into creation and preserved it in Scripture. He is also the God of order, who teaches that love has its proper season and is to be honored, not awakened carelessly. In loving marriage He gives a sign of His own covenant love.

crossReferences.note: The one flesh union of husband and wife that the Song delights in.
Marriage is a profound mystery that refers to Christ and the church.
Rejoice in the wife of your youth; Scripture commends delight in one’s spouse.
The LORD betroths His people to Himself in love, the covenant marriage behind the picture.

study.oneStory: The Song lifts up a gift the world often cheapens and the religious often distrust: the love of a man and a woman. Read within the whole story, that good gift is honored for its own sake and allowed to point higher, to the God who made marriage as a sign and to Christ who loves His bride and will one day bring her home to the marriage supper of the Lamb.

quiz.q: What does the title Song of Songs mean?

options.text: The best or finest of all songs, a Hebrew way of naming the highest of its kind.
options.note: Song of Songs is a superlative, like King of kings, marking it the greatest of songs.

options.text: A song made up of many smaller songs stitched together.
options.note: It does gather many poetic units, but the phrase itself means the finest song.

options.text: A song to be sung only by kings.
options.note: Solomon is named, but the title marks excellence, not a royal audience.

options.text: A song about singing.
options.note: The title is a superlative naming the song’s surpassing worth.

quiz.q: How does the bride describe herself in 1:5 to 6?

options.text: Dark from working in the sun, yet lovely, honest about her weariness but sure she is beautiful.
options.note: She holds together real hardship and true loveliness.

options.text: Beautiful and untroubled, with nothing to confess.
options.note: She does know she is lovely, but the claim that she has nothing to confess misses her open lament over her neglected vineyard.

options.text: Ashamed and unwilling to be seen at all.
options.note: She is genuinely self-conscious about being stared at, yet she is not unwilling to be seen; she still calls herself lovely and longs to be near her beloved.

options.text: Wealthy and idle, kept from all work.
options.note: The opposite: her brothers made her keep the vineyards under the sun.

quiz.q: What is the best way to read the Song as a whole?

options.text: As a real celebration of married love that, because marriage pictures Christ and the church, also points beyond itself.
options.note: The both reading honors the plain sense and lets it point higher.

options.text: Only as an allegory of God’s love, with no reference to human marriage.
options.note: It does picture God’s love, but not by emptying the plain celebration of married love.

options.text: Only as a romance, with no larger meaning in Scripture.
options.note: It truly celebrates married love, yet Scripture ties marriage to Christ and the church.

options.text: As a manual of rules for courtship.
options.note: It is poetry that sings of love, not a rulebook, though it does guard love’s right time.

quiz.q: What is the refrain first sounded in 2:7?

options.text: Do not arouse or awaken love until the time is right.
options.note: This charge, repeated three times, guards love’s proper season.

options.text: A warning to keep love secret forever.
options.note: The refrain does guard love, but by its timing, not by secrecy; the lovers praise each other openly.

options.text: A command never to marry.
options.note: It rightly senses a call to restraint and patience, yet overstates it; the refrain delays love to its proper time, it does not forbid marriage.

options.text: A call to awaken love as quickly as possible.
options.note: It says the opposite: do not awaken love before its time.

quiz.q: How does the dark yet lovely bride point to the gospel?

options.text: She pictures the church, unlovely in herself yet beautiful in her Beloved, desired before she is worthy.
options.note: Christ loves and beautifies a bride who feels dark and overlooked.

options.text: She shows that only the outwardly beautiful are loved.
options.note: The point is the reverse: she is loved though she feels weathered and unworthy.

options.text: She teaches that we must make ourselves lovely before God will draw near.
options.note: The gospel runs the other way: He loves and makes lovely those who are not.

options.text: She has no connection to Christ and the church at all.
options.note: The Song truly celebrates human married love in its own right, but Scripture reads that love as a sign of Christ and His church, Ephesians 5, so it does point beyond itself.

questions: The bride longs openly for her beloved and is unafraid to say so. Where do you long for God, and would you dare to voice that desire as honestly?
She calls herself dark, yet lovely, holding weariness and beauty together. How does it steady you to know Christ calls His people lovely even when they feel worn and overlooked?
His banner over me is love, she says. What would change this week if you truly lived under the banner of Christ’s love rather than under fear or performance?
Three times the Song warns not to awaken love before its time. Where in your life do you need patience to let something good unfold in its proper season?
The Song treats the love of husband and wife as a good and holy gift. How can you honor that gift, whether in your own marriage, in singleness, or in how you speak of others’ marriages?

translationNotes.note: Song of Songs is a Hebrew superlative, the finest of songs, like Holy of Holies or King of kings.
Dark, yet lovely can also be rendered dark and lovely; either way it holds together honest hardship and true beauty.
Do not arouse or awaken love until the time is right is a refrain repeated at 3:5 and 8:4, guarding the proper season of love.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Song of Solomon 1:1-2:7, chapters 1 and 2. Poetic lines rendered as flowing prose within each verse; embedded speaker labels in the source, such as The Friends and The Bride, were treated as headings and not included in the verse text. Every em or en dash in the source was converted to a comma or clean break; none remain.
